title: Asparagus
prose: Asparagus ( Asparagus officinalis ) belongs to the lily family and is marketed in two forms, namely canned or fresh (white and/or green) tips. In South Africa, mainly white asparagus is used. Asparagus is widely grown in South Africa, namely: Free State:Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Modderpoort, Ladybrand, Clocolan, Ficksburg, Fouriesburg, Bethlehem and QwaQwa. Northern Cape: Modder River Station (south of Kimberley) and Vaalharts area. Gauteng:Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Witwatersrand, Vereeniging and Pretoria areas. Â SOIL Deep, well-aerated soil. Loamy sand and sandy loam. A pH (KCl) of approximately 5 to 5.7 is important. Â CLIMATE Asparagus adapts to a variety of climatic conditions. The best results are achieved in areas with very cold, dry winters. Quality is improved with a rest period of 2.5 to 3 months with low temperatures (Strydom et al., 1979). Optimum root and aerial growth is obtained with soil temperatures between 18 Â°C and 29 Â°C. The growth and extension of shoots are controlled by air temperature and not by soil temperature (MacGillivray, 1952). The rainfall in areas of dryland production varies from 600 to 800 mm per annum. Â ESTABLISHMENT Planting time is from August to September, and in warmer areas may be as late as January. Row widths vary from 2.0 to 2.7 metres. Wider rows allow sufficient soil for ridging in later years (Ware and McCollum, 1980). Furrows should be approximately 20 cm below normal soil surface. A fertilizer compound or phosphate is placed at the bottom of the furrow and lightly covered by soil. The crown is placed on top of this soil layer. 12 000 to 18 000 crowns ha -1 are recommended for dryland cultivation. A good stand is very important (Williams and Kendall, 1976; Bussel, 1977; Pinkan et al., 1978). CULTIVATION The soil should be deep and loose. Furrows are made and crowns planted as discussed above. Aerial growth is removed in winter and rows are ridged during August. Flat-topped ridges are ideal to promote asparagus of uniform length. Â HARVESTING Ridges are flattened after harvesting. Tips are harvested as soon as they break through the soil surface. A special, long harvesting knife is used to cut off the shoots at a depth of 20 to 22 cm below the soil surface. According to Takatori et al. (1970), the most shoots and greatest masses are obtained with a harvesting period of 60 days. NUTRIENT REMOVAL Table 5.6.3.1. Removal of nutrients by asparagus Â MACRO-ELEMENTS The recommendations in Commercial Vegetable Recommendations for Michigan (1970) appear to be the most acceptable when compared with those of Dufault et al. (1983), Wessels and Thompson (1937) and even according to the removal figures given by Carolus and Brown (1961). The guidelines do not consider yield targets. Nitrogen (N) Guideline for nitrogen fertilization of asparagus: Establishment: 50 kg N ha -1 Established: 50 to 100 kg N ha -1 per annum. Phosphorus (P) Table 5.6.3.2. Guidelines for P-fertilization of asparagus Potassium (K) Table 5.6.3.3. Guidelines for K-fertilization of asparagus Time of application The most appropriate time of application, for all of the phosphorus and potassium and half of the nitrogen, is just before the levelling of the ridges. Additional nitrogen can be broadcast at a later stage, towards the end of December or the beginning of January, to stimulate maximum growth and to ensure sufficient reserves in the roots for the following cropping season. In a good year, an additional application of 25 to 30 kg N ha -1 can be considered when the second shoots appear. Â ORGANIC FERTILIZATION Organic matter is also important, and for this reason well-composted animal residues and compost can be used successfully. The necessary adjustments should be made regarding the inorganic fertilizer programme. Incorporation of beans, clover, wheat, oats and barley prior to planting is recommended, and the incorporation of crops between rows may also be advantageous. Â REFERENCES BUSSEL, W.T., 1977. Plant density for hand-harvested asparagus. N.Z.J. Agric. (1977), Vol. 135, No. 6, 15, In: Hort. Abstr. (1978), Vol. 48, No. 8, 7309. CAROLUS, R.L. & BROWN, L.D., 1961.
